narration.default: Real apps remember things between runs. Today you'll wrap save and load into reusable functions, then build a tiny menu that lets the kid save a score, load it back, or exit. One short program brings together almost everything you've learned: def, files, while True with break, input, and if-elif chains. This is what real Python feels like — small pieces working as a team.

Before we write any file, tell Python where to put it. Look at the top of the terminal toolbar — there's a button labelled File System. Click it. A folder picker opens — pick any folder on your computer, or make a new one called scores. Click Allow when your browser asks. From this moment on, every file your code saves lands in that real folder on your computer. Open it later and you'll see score.txt sitting there as a real file.

Type the first function:

def save_score(score):
    file = open("score.txt", "w")
    file.write(str(score))
    file.close()

open is standard Python — it asks Python to open a file. The first piece is the filename, the second is the mode. The mode "w" means WRITE — make a fresh file (or replace the old one). file.write puts the score into the file as text — note str(score), because files only hold text. file.close finishes the work and lets Python release the file. Three lines: open, write, close.

Below the functions (no indent), type:

while True:
    print("1. Save")
    print("2. Load")
    print("3. Exit")

    choice = input("Choose: ")

while True is the loop-forever pattern you met with break. Each turn, print a three-option menu, ask the kid to choose. The if/elif/else below will route them to the right action.

Still inside the while, type:

    if choice == "1":
        s = input("Enter score: ")
        save_score(s)

When the kid picks 1, ask for a score and call save_score with it. save_score handles all the file work — this code doesn't need to think about open or close at all. That's the win of wrapping work in a function.

Type the next branch:

    elif choice == "2":
        print("Saved:", load_score())

elif keeps the chain going. When the kid picks 2, call load_score and print whatever it returns. The function does the open-read-close work; this line just shows the result.

Type the last branch:

    elif choice == "3":
        break

break pops out of the while, just like the chat-bot tutorial. Without it the kid would be stuck in the menu forever. With it, picking 3 ends the program cleanly.

Click Run. The menu shows up. Pick 1, type 100 as the score. The menu shows again. Pick 2 — the terminal prints Saved: 100, the value you just stored. Pick 3 to exit. Now open the folder you picked at the start of this tutorial — score.txt is sitting there as a real file with 100 in it. Run again — pick 2 straight away without saving — the file still has 100 from last time.

Look at how much you used in one short program: File System mount to pick a real folder, def to make commands, open + write + close to touch a file, while True with break to keep a menu running, input to ask, if-elif to route, return to hand back the saved value. Seven big ideas working together. The same skeleton — menu loop, save and load functions, a file holding state on disk — is how high-score boards, todo apps, chat histories, and game saves all work behind the scenes.
